name: Media Studies
description: An elective course surveying the history, forms, economics, and cultural influence of mass media from print and radio to digital platforms and media literacy.

modules.title: History of Mass Media
modules.objectives: Trace the development of mass media from the printing press through digital media; explain how each major medium transformed communication and society.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 1.2: The History of Mass Media
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — History of Mass Media Overview
modules.video2Note: YouTube — History of Media: From Cave Paintings to the Internet
modules.practiceNote: Create a timeline of major mass media milestones from 1450 to the present
modules.assignment: Create an annotated timeline of at least eight major media milestones from Gutenberg's printing press to the smartphone era. For each milestone, write 50 words explaining what changed and why it mattered. Then write a 200-word conclusion reflecting on which innovation you believe had the greatest social impact and why.

modules.title: Print Media: Newspapers & Magazines
modules.objectives: Explain the historical and contemporary role of newspapers and magazines; analyze the economic and digital challenges facing print media today.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 4.1: History of Newspapers
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Print Media and Journalism
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The Future of Print Journalism
modules.practiceNote: Compare the front page of a print newspaper with its online edition for differences in framing and emphasis
modules.assignment: Select a major national newspaper and a national magazine. Research their circulation trends over the past 20 years. Write a 350-word analysis explaining: (1) what the data shows about the health of print media, (2) how digital distribution has changed their business model, and (3) whether you believe print journalism has an important future role.

modules.title: Radio & Audio Media
modules.objectives: Trace the development of radio broadcasting; compare traditional radio with podcasting and streaming audio as media formats.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 6.1: History of Radio
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — History of Radio Broadcasting
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The History of Radio and the Birth of Broadcasting
modules.practiceNote: Analyze one podcast episode for its journalistic or entertainment conventions compared to broadcast radio
modules.assignment: Listen to two audio programs: one traditional AM/FM radio broadcast and one podcast on a similar topic. Write a 350-word comparison analyzing differences in production style, audience interaction, advertising model, and accessibility. Conclude with your view on which format better serves the public interest.

modules.title: Television
modules.objectives: Explain television's role as a dominant mass medium; analyze how television programming reflects and shapes social values and culture.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 7.1: History of Television
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — History of Television as a Medium
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How Television Changed the World
modules.practiceNote: Apply cultivation theory by comparing a TV drama's depiction of crime with actual crime statistics
modules.assignment: Choose a popular television genre (reality TV, crime drama, sitcom, or news) and watch two episodes. Write a 400-word critical analysis: identify the social groups represented, examine how they are portrayed (stereotyped, realistic, absent?), and explain what values or worldview the program implicitly promotes to its audience.

modules.title: Film as Medium
modules.objectives: Analyze film as a medium of communication and cultural expression; identify how cinematic techniques convey meaning and ideology.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 8.1: History of Film
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Film Theory
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Film as a Communication Medium: How Movies Persuade
modules.practiceNote: Analyze a 5-minute film scene for cinematography, editing, and narrative choices that convey meaning
modules.assignment: Watch a film scene of your choice (at least 3 minutes). Write a 400-word film analysis examining: (1) how camera angles and shots establish power relationships between characters, (2) how music or sound design shapes your emotional response, and (3) what ideological messages the scene communicates about society, gender, or class.

modules.title: Online & Digital Media
modules.objectives: Explain how the internet transformed media production, distribution, and consumption; evaluate both opportunities and risks of digital media.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 11.1: The Internet and Social Media
modules.video2Note: YouTube — What is the Internet? (How it Works)
modules.practiceNote: Evaluate three online news sources for credibility markers using fact-checking criteria
modules.assignment: Select three different websites covering the same news story (a traditional newspaper website, a news aggregator, and a social media post). Write a 350-word comparison analyzing: how each platform frames the story, what information is emphasized or missing, which sources are cited, and which version you find most credible and why.

modules.title: Social Media Platforms
modules.objectives: Describe the major social media platforms and their distinctive communication features; analyze how platform design shapes user behavior and content.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 11.2: Social Media
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — The World Wide Web and Social Platforms
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How Social Media Platforms Manipulate Your Behavior
modules.practiceNote: Create a platform comparison chart analyzing audience, format, monetization, and communication style for three social media platforms
modules.assignment: Choose two social media platforms with very different designs (e.g., TikTok vs. LinkedIn). Write a 400-word analysis explaining: how each platform's design features (character limits, visual focus, algorithm, verification) shape what users communicate, who communicates, and how audiences respond.

modules.title: Journalism & News Production
modules.objectives: Explain the principles of professional journalism (accuracy, fairness, independence); trace how news is produced and how digital media has transformed journalism.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 4.2: How Newspapers Work
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Journalism Basics and News Standards
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How News Is Made: The Journalism Process
modules.practiceNote: Evaluate a news article using the Society of Professional Journalists Code of Ethics
modules.assignment: Find a news story covered by at least three different outlets (one traditional newspaper, one broadcast network, one digital-native outlet). Write a 400-word analysis comparing: the headline choices, what sources each outlet consulted, what information each emphasized, and any evidence of bias or ethical lapses in any version.

quizQuestions.question: Which invention by Johannes Gutenberg in the 1450s is considered the first major milestone in mass media history?
options: The telegraph
The movable type printing press
The radio transmitter
The daguerreotype photograph
quizQuestions.explanation: Gutenberg's movable type printing press (~1450) made mass production of texts possible, democratizing information and launching the era of mass communication.

quizQuestions.question: McLuhan's famous phrase 'the medium is the message' means:
options: The content of a message is more important than how it is delivered
The form of a medium itself shapes the content and impact of communication
Television is a more powerful medium than radio
Mass media exists only to sell advertising
quizQuestions.explanation: McLuhan argued that the medium itself — its form and structure — shapes perception and society independently of the specific content it carries.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which invention by Johannes Gutenberg in the 1450s is considered the first major milestone in mass media history" is: B.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.explanation: True. B is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Fill in the blank: Complete: In the context of the topic, ___ refers to "the transition from 'web 1.0' to 'web 2.0' is significant in media history because web 2.0 enabled:."
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: B.

quizQuestions.question: The term 'penny press' (1830s) refers to newspapers that:
options: Were funded entirely by government subsidies
Were cheaply priced and aimed at a mass general audience
Covered only financial and business news
Were published by political parties for their supporters
quizQuestions.explanation: The penny press made newspapers affordable (one cent) to working-class readers for the first time, transforming newspapers into a true mass medium with wide circulation.

quizQuestions.question: Investigative journalism that exposes corruption and social problems is known as:
options: Yellow journalism
Muckraking
Sensationalism
Public relations
quizQuestions.explanation: Muckraking (early 1900s) was investigative journalism that exposed corruption, corporate misconduct, and social injustice, driving important social reforms.

quizQuestions.question: The primary economic model for most print newspapers historically has been:
options: Government grants and public funding
Subscription and advertising revenue
Individual donor contributions
Pay-per-article micropayments
quizQuestions.explanation: Newspapers have traditionally relied on a dual revenue model: paid subscriptions from readers and display/classified advertising from businesses.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The term 'penny press' (1830s) refers to newspapers that:" is: B.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.explanation: True. B is correct.

quizQuestions.question: The 'Golden Age of Radio' (1920s–1950s) refers to a period when radio:
options: Was used exclusively by the military
Became the dominant home entertainment medium in America
Was available only to wealthy households
Primarily broadcast political news and nothing else
quizQuestions.explanation: The Golden Age of Radio was a period when radio was the primary entertainment medium in American homes, featuring dramas, comedies, news, and sports.

quizQuestions.question: What distinguishes a podcast from traditional broadcast radio?
options: Podcasts can only be listened to on smartphones
Podcasts are on-demand recordings accessible anytime without a broadcast schedule
Podcasts are always live and interactive
Podcasts must be licensed by the FCC like broadcast radio
quizQuestions.explanation: Podcasts are recorded audio programs available on-demand at any time through the internet, unlike broadcast radio which requires tuning in at a scheduled time.

quizQuestions.question: The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) regulates broadcast radio primarily by:
options: Setting the prices broadcasters must charge for advertising
Licensing radio stations and enforcing content standards on public airwaves
Owning and operating all public radio stations
Requiring all radio stations to broadcast news programs
quizQuestions.explanation: The FCC grants licenses to radio broadcasters to use the public airwaves and enforces regulations including content standards, ownership rules, and equal time provisions.

quizQuestions.question: Fill in the blank: Complete: In the context of the topic, ___ refers to "national public radio (npr) is an example of:."
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: B.

quizQuestions.question: Cultivation Theory, developed by George Gerbner, suggests that heavy television viewing:
options: Makes viewers more politically informed and active
Shapes viewers' perception of reality to match television's version of the world
Has no measurable effect on attitudes or beliefs
Primarily affects children under 10 but not adults
quizQuestions.explanation: Cultivation Theory argues that long-term, heavy TV viewers develop a perception of social reality (e.g., crime, gender roles) that reflects what they see on television rather than actual statistics.

quizQuestions.question: The 'mean world syndrome,' associated with Cultivation Theory, refers to the belief that:
options: Media violence directly causes real-world violent behavior
Heavy TV viewers believe the world is more dangerous than it actually is
Children who watch violent TV become desensitized to violence
News programs exaggerate crime to attract larger audiences
quizQuestions.explanation: Gerbner found that heavy TV viewers, exposed to disproportionate amounts of crime and violence on TV, tend to perceive the real world as more dangerous than it is.

quizQuestions.question: Which regulatory body in the United States oversees broadcast television content?
options: The FTC (Federal Trade Commission)
The FCC (Federal Communications Commission)
The FEC (Federal Election Commission)
The FCA (Federal Content Authority)
quizQuestions.explanation: The FCC regulates broadcast television in the US, including licensing stations, enforcing content standards (indecency, equal time rules), and overseeing ownership.

quizQuestions.question: The television industry's shift to streaming services (Netflix, Hulu, Disney+) is significant because streaming:
options: Requires government licensing like broadcast television
Operates on a subscription model outside traditional broadcast regulation
Can only deliver content that previously aired on broadcast TV
Is exclusively available in major metropolitan areas
quizQuestions.explanation: Streaming services operate via the internet and subscription fees outside the broadcast regulatory framework, allowing more content freedom and a different economic model than traditional TV.

quizQuestions.question: The Bechdel Test for films measures whether a film:
options: Passes standards of MPAA rating appropriateness
Features at least two women who talk to each other about something other than a man
Has a female director or producer in a leadership role
Contains no offensive stereotypes of any social group
quizQuestions.explanation: The Bechdel Test (named after cartoonist Alison Bechdel) measures female representation by checking if a film has two named women characters who speak to each other about something besides a man.

quizQuestions.question: The Hollywood studio system, dominant from the 1920s–1950s, was characterized by:
options: Independent filmmakers having full creative control
Studios controlling all aspects of production, distribution, and exhibition
Government ownership of all major film production companies
Films being produced solely for overseas audiences
quizQuestions.explanation: The Hollywood studio system featured major studios (MGM, Warner Bros., Paramount) controlling the entire film industry from production through distribution and theater ownership.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The Bechdel Test for films measures whether a film:" is: B.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.explanation: True. B is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The Hollywood studio system, dominant from the 1920s–1950s, was characterized by:" is: Independent filmmakers having full creative control.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: B.

quizQuestions.question: The World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989–1991 primarily to:
options: Create a platform for social media and personal networking
Enable researchers to share information via hyperlinked documents
Provide a commercial marketplace for online shopping
Allow governments to communicate securely
quizQuestions.explanation: Berners-Lee invented the Web at CERN as a system for researchers to share scientific information using hypertext documents connected by links.

quizQuestions.question: Digital convergence refers to the merging of:
options: Print and broadcast media companies into single corporations
Previously distinct media technologies into unified digital platforms
All internet traffic through a single government server
News and entertainment programming into a single format
quizQuestions.explanation: Digital convergence describes how formerly separate media forms (text, audio, video, telephone) have merged into integrated digital platforms accessible on single devices.

quizQuestions.question: Net neutrality refers to the principle that:
options: Internet users should only post neutral, non-partisan content
Internet service providers should treat all internet traffic equally without discrimination
Social media platforms should remain neutral in political debates
Only government-approved content can be hosted on the internet
quizQuestions.explanation: Net neutrality is the principle that ISPs must treat all internet traffic equally, not slowing, blocking, or prioritizing certain content, services, or websites over others.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989–1991 primarily to:" is: B.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.explanation: True. B is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Fill in the blank: Complete: In the context of the topic, ___ refers to "an 'echo chamber' online is problematic for democratic discourse because it:."
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: B.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best describes how social media algorithms determine what content to show users?
options: Content is shown chronologically and equally to all followers
Algorithms prioritize content likely to generate the most engagement based on past user behavior
Content is curated by professional editors who select quality posts
Only verified accounts and official news sources appear prominently
quizQuestions.explanation: Social media algorithms use machine learning to optimize for engagement, prioritizing content similar to what users have previously liked, shared, or spent time viewing.

quizQuestions.question: The 'attention economy' in social media refers to the idea that:
options: Advertisers pay users directly for their attention
User attention is the primary resource platforms compete for and sell to advertisers
Social media companies pay content creators based on views
Governments regulate how much attention media can demand from users
quizQuestions.explanation: In the attention economy, social media platforms compete to capture and hold user attention, which is then sold to advertisers — making user attention the fundamental commodity.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following best describes how social media algorithms determine what content to show users" is: B.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.explanation: True. B is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Fill in the blank: Complete: In the context of the topic, ___ refers to "social media platforms generate the majority of their revenue through:."
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: B.

quizQuestions.question: The journalistic principle of 'accuracy' requires reporters to:
options: Publish a story as quickly as possible to beat competitors
Verify facts through multiple independent sources before publishing
Report only information that is favorable to the public interest
Include only information that can be proven beyond any doubt
quizQuestions.explanation: Accuracy, a cornerstone of journalistic ethics, requires journalists to verify facts through reliable, independent sources before publication.

quizQuestions.question: The inverted pyramid structure in news writing organizes information by placing:
options: Background information first, then main facts, then conclusion
Most important information first, followed by decreasing levels of detail
Chronological events in the order they occurred
The journalist's analysis at the top, followed by supporting quotes
quizQuestions.explanation: The inverted pyramid places the most newsworthy information (who, what, when, where, why) first, with supporting details and background following in descending order of importance.

quizQuestions.question: Citizen journalism refers to:
options: Journalism produced only by professionally trained reporters
News content created and distributed by non-professional members of the public
Government-funded news organizations with public service mandates
Journalism that focuses exclusively on local community issues
quizQuestions.explanation: Citizen journalism involves ordinary members of the public collecting, reporting, and publishing news — made possible by smartphones and social media.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The journalistic principle of 'accuracy' requires reporters to:" is: B.
options: True
False
quizQuestions.explanation: True. B is correct.

questions.question: Marshall McLuhan's phrase 'the medium is the message' means:
options: Content is always more important than delivery format
The medium of communication itself shapes meaning and social impact
Television is a superior medium to print
All media deliver identical messages regardless of format
questions.explanation: McLuhan argued that the structure and characteristics of a medium transform how people perceive and understand information, independent of the specific content delivered.

questions.question: The 'penny press' newspapers of the 1830s are historically significant because they:
options: Were the first newspapers to use the telegraph for news gathering
Made newspapers affordable to the working class for the first time
Were published entirely by women journalists
Focused exclusively on political news without advertising
questions.explanation: The penny press revolution made newspapers cheap enough for ordinary working-class readers, expanding the audience for newspapers dramatically and establishing the advertising-supported model.

questions.question: The FCC's primary role in American broadcasting is to:
options: Produce public service announcements for broadcast networks
License broadcast stations and enforce content and ownership regulations
Set advertising rates for commercial television and radio
Operate the national public broadcasting system (PBS and NPR)
questions.explanation: The FCC regulates broadcast media by issuing licenses to use the public airwaves and enforcing rules on content, ownership limits, and equal time for political candidates.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Cultivation Theory proposes that television's most significant long-term effect on audiences is:" is: B.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: True. B is correct.

questions.question: The Bechdel Test measures:
options: A film's MPAA rating appropriateness for different audiences
Whether a film has two women characters who talk to each other about something other than a man
The proportion of female filmmakers in the industry
Whether a film passes standards set by feminist critics
questions.explanation: The Bechdel Test is a minimal standard of female representation in film, checking whether at least two named women characters have a conversation about something other than a man.

questions.question: Net neutrality refers to the principle that:
options: Internet content must remain politically neutral
ISPs must treat all internet traffic equally without preferential treatment
Social media platforms must provide equal visibility to all political views
Internet users must register their real identities online
questions.explanation: Net neutrality requires Internet Service Providers to treat all data traffic equally, preventing them from creating 'fast lanes' for paying companies or blocking competitors.

questions.question: Context collapse on social media means:
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: B.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "The inverted pyramid structure in journalism places:" is: Opinion and analysis at the top of the article.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: B.

questions.question: Native advertising is best described as:
options: Advertising created by and for indigenous communities
Paid promotional content designed to look like editorial content
Outdoor advertising on billboards and public spaces
Advertising that targets consumers based on geographic location
questions.explanation: Native advertising is a form of paid advertising designed to match the look, format, and tone of the surrounding editorial content, blurring the line between advertising and journalism.

questions.question: Vertical integration in the media industry refers to:
options: A company hiring journalists at various levels of seniority
A company owning multiple stages of production, distribution, and exhibition
Media companies expanding into international markets
Journalists rising from junior to senior editorial roles
questions.explanation: Vertical integration occurs when one company controls multiple stages of the value chain — for example, owning a film studio, distribution company, and theater chain simultaneously.

questions.question: The male gaze, as described by Laura Mulvey, refers to:
options: The tendency of male audiences to prefer action films over romance
Mainstream cinema's tendency to present women from a male heterosexual perspective
Camera techniques that show characters making direct eye contact with the viewer
The formal requirement that male actors appear in all major roles
questions.explanation: Mulvey's influential theory argued that Hollywood cinema is structured around a male heterosexual gaze that objectifies women and positions viewers to identify with male protagonists.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "The First Amendment protects press freedom primarily by:" is: B.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: True. B is correct.

questions.question: Glocalization in media refers to:
options: Restricting foreign media to protect domestic culture
Adapting global media content to fit local cultural tastes and contexts
Creating media content that appeals equally to all global audiences
Governments partnering with international media companies
questions.explanation: Glocalization describes how global media companies adapt their content, products, or formats to resonate with local cultural preferences while maintaining global distribution.

questions.question: Media literacy requires consumers to evaluate media for which of the following?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: B.

questions.question: The rise of podcasting has disrupted traditional radio broadcasting primarily by:
options: Making broadcast radio illegal in most markets
Offering on-demand, niche content outside the FCC broadcast licensing system
Replacing all music content with spoken-word programming
Requiring listeners to pay monthly subscription fees for radio access
questions.explanation: Podcasting allows creators to publish audio content directly to listeners on-demand without broadcast licenses, FCC oversight, or traditional radio distribution infrastructure.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Web 2.0 transformed the internet primarily by:" is: Making internet access faster through improved infrastructure.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: B.

questions.question: Social media algorithms primarily optimize for:
options: Accuracy and credibility of shared information
User engagement metrics (likes, shares, time spent)
Diversity of viewpoints in each user's feed
Content from verified professional news organizations
questions.explanation: Social media algorithms are designed to maximize engagement — showing content most likely to generate reactions, shares, and longer viewing time — rather than optimizing for accuracy or diversity.

questions.question: The 'mean world syndrome' associated with Cultivation Theory refers to:
options: The tendency of news media to report only negative events
Heavy TV viewers believing the real world is more dangerous than it actually is
The desensitization to violence caused by video game exposure
Social media's tendency to amplify angry and hostile comments
questions.explanation: Mean world syndrome describes how heavy viewers of crime-heavy television develop the perception that the world is more dangerous and crime-ridden than actual statistics indicate.

questions.question: Editorial independence in journalism is threatened when:
options: Journalists disagree with each other's assessments of stories
Owners or advertisers pressure reporters to change or kill stories that harm their interests
Reporters must have stories reviewed by editors before publication
News organizations publish opinion columns alongside news reporting
questions.explanation: Editorial independence is undermined when commercial or political interests — rather than journalistic judgment — determine which stories get published and how they are framed.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Defamation requires a plaintiff to prove which of the following elements" is: B.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: True. B is correct.

questions.question: Cultural imperialism in global media is most associated with the concern that:
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: B.

questions.question: Intersectionality in media analysis examines how:
options: Multiple media platforms can be accessed simultaneously
Overlapping identities (race, gender, class) interact to shape representation and experience
News organizations must cover all sides of a controversy
Advertising targets people based on combinations of demographic factors
questions.explanation: Intersectionality (Crenshaw) is the framework for understanding how race, gender, class, sexuality, and other identities overlap and interact, creating distinct experiences of privilege and oppression reflected in media.

questions.question: The attention economy model of social media means that:
options: Social media users must pay for professional attention from influencers
Platforms generate profit by capturing and selling user attention to advertisers
Only verified accounts receive significant platform attention
Social media companies pay users for their time on the platform
questions.explanation: In the attention economy, platforms capture user attention and sell it to advertisers — making the platform's core product not the content but the users' time and attention.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "The FTC requires influencers to disclose sponsored content primarily because:" is: Influencers earn more money when they disclose paid partnerships.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: B.

questions.question: The difference between misinformation and disinformation is that disinformation is:
options: Less harmful because it spreads more slowly
Deliberately false content intended to deceive or manipulate
Found only on social media rather than traditional media
Always created by government actors or foreign states
questions.explanation: Disinformation is intentionally false content created to deceive, while misinformation is false content spread by people who believe it is true. Intent is the key distinguishing factor.

questions.question: SIFT as a media literacy strategy requires users first to:
options: Search for the most popular version of a story
Stop and resist the impulse to share or react before evaluating the source
Find the original source publication of a claim
Trust content from accounts with verified badges
questions.explanation: The first step of SIFT is to Stop — pause before reacting or sharing to give yourself time to think critically rather than sharing on impulse.

questions.question: New York Times v. Sullivan (1964) established that public officials suing for defamation must prove:
options: That the newspaper had a profit motive for publishing the story
Actual malice — knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard for truth
That the story was published without any fact-checking
That at least three people read and believed the false statement
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court's landmark ruling created the actual malice standard, giving press significant protection when covering public officials by requiring proof of intentional or reckless falsehood.

questions.question: Media consolidation is most likely to harm public interest by:
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: B.

questions.question: Lateral reading as a fact-checking strategy involves:
options: Reading an article multiple times to understand it thoroughly
Checking what other credible sources say about a source rather than judging it solely on its own claims
Reading only the headline and first paragraph of online articles
Comparing the article to Wikipedia for background information only
questions.explanation: Lateral reading — the technique used by professional fact-checkers — involves leaving a website immediately and searching for what others say about the source's credibility.

questions.question: Media representation of social groups matters for society primarily because:
options: Legal regulations require proportional representation in media
Repeated representations shape stereotypes, social norms, and how groups are perceived and treated
Only young children are influenced by media portrayals of social groups
Diverse representation always improves media ratings and profits
questions.explanation: Research consistently shows that consistent media stereotypes and representation patterns shape audience beliefs, social norms, career aspirations, and perceptions of different groups.

questions.question: The 24-hour news cycle has most significantly changed journalism by:
options: Giving reporters more time for thorough investigation
Creating pressure to publish quickly, sometimes at the cost of accuracy and depth
Eliminating the distinction between news and opinion content
Making newspapers obsolete and replacing them with television news
questions.explanation: Continuous news cycles pressure reporters to publish immediately, sometimes leading to errors, incomplete information, and prioritizing speed over thoroughness.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "The concept of media as the 'fourth estate' reflects the press's role as:" is: The fourth largest sector in the US economy.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: B.

questions.question: Al Jazeera's significance in global media is primarily that it:
options: Was the first television network to broadcast in HD globally
Offered a non-Western perspective that challenged Western media's dominance in international news
Became the world's largest social media company
Was the first network to live-stream major international events online
questions.explanation: Al Jazeera (1996) provided an Arab/Global South perspective on international events, diversifying the landscape of international news previously dominated by CNN, BBC, and other Western outlets.

questions.question: Copyright law's 'fair use' doctrine allows limited use of copyrighted material for which purposes?
options: Any use that is not commercially profitable
Criticism, commentary, education, news reporting, and parody under specified conditions
Any use by nonprofit organizations
Any use that credits the original creator in the text
questions.explanation: Fair use permits limited use of copyrighted material for purposes including criticism, education, news reporting, and parody, evaluated by four factors including purpose, nature, amount, and market effect.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a media literate person's response to persuasive advertising?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: B.
